ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for a Singaporean pork chop and egg sandwich. FOODON_00002473 (sandwich food product) is used as the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists for this specific composite dish. A0FKP (Pig meat products) is used as the closest defensible parent. The dish also contains egg and bread components not captured by this code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT food concept found for this specific composite Singaporean sandwich dish.

serving.description: 1 sandwich (approximately 2 slices of toast with pork chop and egg)
serving.natural_language_portion: One serving is approximately 220 g, consisting of two slices of toasted white bread, one fried pork loin cutlet (approximately 120 g), and one fried egg, as typically served at Singaporean kopitiam establishments such as Toast Box.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated composite from HPB Singapore Foodo and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Pork loin (fried), fried egg, white toast bread
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB Foodo) and USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single HPB Foodo or USDA entry exists for this exact composite dish. Values are estimated by weighted combination of three components per 100 g of assembled sandwich: fried pork loin cutlet (~55% by weight, HPB Foodo / USDA SR Legacy pork loin pan-fried), fried egg (~20% by weight, USDA FDC 173424 egg whole fried), and toasted white bread (~25% by weight, USDA FDC 325871 bread white commercially prepared toasted). Macros blended proportionally. Sodium elevated to reflect typical kopitiam seasoning and marinade. Values represent a reasonable central estimate for a standard 220 g serving.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of white bread and mixed sandwich analogues; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2008) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a Singaporean pork chop and egg sandwich. GI estimated at 55 based on the dominant carbohydrate vehicle being white toast bread (GI ~70 per University of Sydney GI Database) moderated by the substantial protein (pork, egg) and fat content of the filling, which is well-established in the literature to reduce the overall glycemic response of mixed meals. The composite GI of 55 is consistent with published mixed-meal sandwich analogues in Atkinson et al. (2008). Glycemic load per serving calculated as (55 × 40.7 g available carbohydrate per 220 g serving) / 100 = 22.4.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~480 mg per 100 g; ~1056 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this sandwich due to its high sodium load (~1056 mg per 220 g serving, representing approximately 46% of the WHO recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg), primarily from seasoned fried pork, marinade, and processed bread.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~22.4 per serving) driven by white toast bread as the carbohydrate base
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this sandwich with caution; the white toast bread contributes a moderate glycemic load of approximately 22.4 per serving, and portion control combined with pairing with non-starchy vegetables or a lower-GI bread alternative is advisable to manage postprandial blood glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~3.6 g per 100 g) and elevated sodium from deep-fried pork cutlet
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of this sandwich due to its moderate saturated fat content from deep-fried pork and egg, combined with high sodium levels, both of which are associated with increased cardiovascular risk; choosing grilled pork and a reduced-sodium preparation would lower the risk profile.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pork Chop with Egg Sandwich is a popular Singaporean kopitiam-style sandwich consisting of a crispy deep-fried or pan-fried pork loin cutlet and a fried egg, served between two slices of freshly toasted white bread. It is a signature item at establishments such as Toast Box, a well-known Singaporean toast café chain. The pork cutlet is typically marinated in soy sauce, garlic, and spices before frying, yielding a golden, crispy exterior with a juicy, tender interior. The fried egg adds richness and protein, while the toasted bread provides a light crunch and mild sweetness. The overall flavour profile is savoury, slightly smoky, and satisfying, making it a popular breakfast, brunch, or light meal option. Nutritionally, a 220 g serving provides approximately 546 kcal, 35.6 g protein, 28.2 g fat, and 40.7 g carbohydrate. The dish is relatively high in sodium (~1056 mg per serving) due to the seasoned pork and processed bread, which is a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The moderate glycemic load (~22.4) from white toast bread requires attention for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those following halal or kosher diets due to the pork content. It is a culturally significant comfort food in Singapore's kopitiam dining culture.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this exact composite Singaporean kopitiam sandwich. Nutrient values are estimated by proportional blending of three component foods from HPB Foodo Singapore and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (fried pork loin, fried egg, toasted white bread). GI is an analogous estimate based on white bread GI moderated by protein and fat content per published mixed-meal literature. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component foods are well-characterised in approved sources and the blending methodology is standard practice for composite dishes.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from HPB Foodo Singapore and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data; no direct laboratory analysis of this specific dish is available in approved sources. 2. Sodium value (~480 mg/100 g) is elevated above the raw-ingredient blend to reflect typical kopitiam marinade and seasoning practices; this is an informed estimate. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, and all B-vitamins are estimated from component proportions and are reported as best estimates rather than confirmed values; these fields are not -1 because plausible estimates are available from the component sources. 4. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate; direct GI measurement of this dish is not available in the University of Sydney GI Database. 5. The dish contains pork and is therefore not halal or kosher. 6. Serving size of 220 g is an estimate based on typical Toast Box portion descriptions; actual weight may vary by restaurant. 7. FoodEx2 code A0FKP and FoodOn FOODON_00002473 are closest-parent matches; no exact ontology code exists for this specific dish.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Built full composite nutrient estimate from HPB Foodo and USDA SR Legacy component data for pork loin (fried), fried egg, and toasted white bread.
Iteration 1: Established GI of 55 as analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database white bread GI moderated by protein/fat content per Atkinson et al. (2008).
Iteration 1: Added all five mandatory Singaporean language names (English via food_name, Chinese simplified, Chinese traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi).
Iteration 1: Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases list for exact string consistency across all 18 alias entries.

Iteration 1: Confirmed all per_100g values are numeric; no -1 sentinel values used as plausible estimates were available from component sources; all ontology match_type values use only allowed strings (exact, closest_parent, none).

Iteration 2: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' to accurately reflect the glycemic_index of 55, which falls within the low GI range (<= 55) as per University of Sydney GI Database guidelines. This resolves the Q4 failure from the previous iteration.
